<!-- Topic 4: C-Strings -->
<!-- Total slides: 7 -->

# C-Strings

---

## What was a string before std::string? {.smaller}

- A C-string is a `char` array where the last element is the null terminator `'\0'`
- The terminator tells functions where the string ends — without it, nothing works correctly

::: notes
Total slides: 7
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## The null terminator

```
char name[6] = {'A', 'l', 'i', 'c', 'e', '\0'};
```

```
index:  0    1    2    3    4    5
value: 'A'  'l'  'i'  'c'  'e'  '\0'
```

- The array must be at least one element longer than the text it holds
- `'\0'` has integer value 0 — it is not the character `'0'`

::: notes
[Graphic suggestion: a six-cell array with each character labeled above its index; the last cell highlighted in a different color containing '\0' with the label "null terminator"]
:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Declaration options

```{.cpp}
char name1[6] = "Alice";        // compiler adds '\0'
char name2[]  = "Alice";        // size inferred: 6 (5 chars + '\0')
char name3[6];                  // uninitialized — garbage + no terminator
```

- String literal initialization is the safe path — the compiler places `'\0'` automatically
- An uninitialized char array has no terminator; using it as a string is undefined behavior

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Input and output

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
#include <cstring>
using namespace std;

char name[50];

cout << "Enter name: ";
cin >> name;            // reads until whitespace; adds '\0'
cout << name << "\n";   // cout stops at '\0'
```

- `cin >>` stops at the first whitespace and always adds `'\0'`
- The array must be large enough — `cin >>` does not check bounds

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## Functions from \<cstring\>

```{.cpp}
char a[] = "Hello";
char b[] = "World";
char c[20];

strlen(a);          // 5 — characters before '\0', not counting it
strcpy(c, a);       // copies a into c, including '\0'
strcat(c, b);       // appends b onto c: "HelloWorld"
strcmp(a, b);       // < 0: a < b, 0: equal, > 0: a > b
```

- All functions depend on `'\0'` — a missing terminator causes undefined behavior
- Always make the destination array large enough before calling `strcpy` or `strcat`

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## C-strings vs std::string

| | C-string (`char[]`) | `std::string` |
|---|---|---|
| Header | `<cstring>` | `<string>` |
| Null terminator | Required, manual | Managed automatically |
| Length | `strlen()` | `.length()` |
| Assignment | `strcpy()` | `=` |
| Concatenation | `strcat()` | `+` |
| Bounds checking | None | Via `.at()` |

- Prefer `std::string` for new code; C-strings appear in legacy code and C library interfaces

<!-- Slide 6 -->

---

## Summary

- A C-string is a `char` array terminated by `'\0'` — the terminator is what makes it a string
- Use string literal initialization and `<cstring>` functions; prefer `std::string` whenever possible

<!-- Slide 7 -->
